symptomSummary: After a prompt revision accumulates ~30+ invocations, the evolve loop generates regressions by addressing old failures instead of recently-flagged ones.
rootCauseHypothesis: The repository layer fetches all historical invocations or signals for a revision without any recency constraint (no ORDER BY timestamp DESC + LIMIT, no time-decay weighting), so as total invocations grow the older, more numerous records statistically dominate the feedback sample passed to the evolution prompt.
fixHypothesis: The query that retrieves invocations or negative signals for a given prompt revision should be windowed to the N most recent records (e.g. ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20) or supplemented with a recency weight, so the evolution prompt is constructed from recent signal rather than the full historical aggregate.

primaryLocation.rationale: This file was changed in recent commits and is the data-access layer for invocations and signals. Threshold-shaped failures (works fine at low volume, breaks at scale) are a canonical symptom of a missing LIMIT/ORDER-BY in a query — as rows accumulate the unweighted aggregate shifts toward older data. The evolve pipeline must call into this layer to retrieve feedback; if the query returns all rows unsorted, recency is lost before it ever reaches the evolution logic.
primaryLocation.function: getInvocations or getSignals (or equivalent fetch for a revision's feedback)

candidateLocations.rationale: This service orchestrates evolution and aggregates feedback records into the LLM prompt. Even if the repository returns all rows, a simple Array.join/map here without recency filtering equally causes old signals to dominate.
The main public evolve entry-point may contain feedback-collection logic directly rather than fully delegating to the service layer.
The API route may pass (or omit) a limit/since-timestamp parameter to the evolution service; if unbounded, this is the configuration site of the bug.
A second store abstraction between the SQLite repositories and evolution service that may impose its own unbounded fetch.
